item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image is from here and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's juuuust enough pieces that fit together for this to create a single coherent whole. I know it was not intended for there to be a clear answer, but here goes:

+ *potential spoiler*
- hide

[I'm certain I'm not the first to string this together, just the first to hold up my little preschool-art-project and demand attention.
The concealed "█████ █████" is almost certainly North Korea. Since the peninsula hasn't been divided for even a century, the imbalance must be anomalous. Easy answer:SCP-1427 has been affecting the entire nation. This next bit might just be my head-canon (since SCP-1427 doesn't have a daevite tag, but I always assumed the 6-fingered oppressor race was the Daeva (or theosophical gibborim if you prefer), but the highest concentration of 1719-1 being in an area being subjugated by a daevite artifact seems like a REALLY bad sign.
Now consider that

Approximately 45% of humanoid subjects held by the Foundation exhibiting other anomalies are, in addition, SCP-1719-1 specimens.

How much of the anomalous is daeva-related?!?!?!
It also puts a nice spin on the material from The First Visionary Church of the Revelation.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So one part They Live, one part Bart Vs The Space Mutants, and two different parts His Dark Materials? Interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is no apparent correlation between SCP-1719-1 specimens and SCP-1237-1-positive individuals.

… I can't see the reason to bring that up then.

Is almost like saying "we found no correlation between this and cheese preference on pizza". Basically adds nothing to the information.

item.title: Secondary Effect
item.content:encoded:
Am I getting a whiff of completely irrational paranoia from the Foundation here?

The extensive research has shown absolutely no evidence of anything additionally anomalous and/or aggressive accounts among -1s. The more we investigate, the less we see any clandestine correlation, common co-operation, or criminal conspiracy.

Given the large body of evidence to the contrary, further analysis would be considered less than urgent, but it is made a "class 1 research priority." Additionally, the Foundation has already fast-tracked measures to upgrade it to keter and plans to join forces with their enemies, the GOC, and developed a plan to exterminate one tenth of the world's population.

This is f███ing bonkers, right? The Foundation is prudently paranoid, given the shit it has to deal with, but they are coldly rational above all else. Maybe that's why this reads like subtly hostile incredulity and restrained reactionary aggression. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

A couple crosslinks could be added to this article in the section that discusses the Summary of Research. First of all, there is a blackboxed SCP, which could be filled in. SCP-1972-A fits this blackboxing, and looks similar to what an SCP-1719-1 instance looks like. Therefore, it'd be interesting for SCP-1972-A to look like a normal human. (EDIT: eric_h has also positively identified 1972-A as the blackboxed skip on Page 2 of this SCP's discussion)

Editing this:

  • SCP-████-█ appears to be a normal human when viewed through SCP-1719. The significance of this is, again, unclear.

To this:

  • SCP-1972-A appears to be a normal human when viewed through SCP-1719. The significance of this is, again, unclear.

Additionally, this article deals with a (possibly) genetic phenomenon present in a large percentage of the human population. There is another anomaly like this, being SCP-1237, which can be crosslinked.
Suggesting adding this after the second bullet point:

  • There is no apparent correlation between SCP-1719-1 specimens and SCP-1237-1-positive individuals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading this is an interesting experience after watching Devilman Crybaby.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap 11.4% of us are demons, take my upvote pls.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My theory. The -1 are normal people. The lens randomly picks based on {?} and a random number generator whether a person will be shown funny. We are losing 11% of agents and D-class for nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish there was something more thoughtful for me to say than "this was an enjoyable, high-concept goof with enough ambiguity to make it interesting." But it was, so there isn't.

+1 for that Series II feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That SCP appearing to be a normal human line made me choose the option A. "This is alien shit"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man. This skip leaves just enough out to leave me gagging to know more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My pocket theory is that -1s are not the weird ones here, the rest of the world is.

The impostors who are too good at being impostors.

Either way, great article, aside from the note, which adds nothing.

item.title: YAY
item.content:encoded: *ENTHUSIASTICALLY APPLAUDS*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We are legion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, the note at the end sort of ruins it for me. Sure, it could just be that the writer of the note is crazy or something, but it removes the sense of mystery that made me adore this article. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *thumbs-up*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had never seen those before, but yes, some of those are how I imagine SCP-1719-1s looking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes me think of the paintings of Arcimboldo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good lord, it turns the population of Earth into the Stanford Prisoner Experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As it is, it's kind of vague—I mean, you mention cetacean and procyonids, both of which aren't that uncommon as fursonas, and I know several raccoon therians.

Honestly, though, I don't think it should matter too much. The vast majority like it.

(As a side note, furries are not the same as therians/otherkin. Furries are people who have an interest in anthropomorphized animals. Therians are people who, in terms of animalistic behaviors, are on the far end of the bell curve, and generally have them match up with a single species.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was not seriously considering the "furry" aspect of this thing, and didn't think it would register that way because I imagine the SCP-1719-1s to look completely horrible to most people. How many furries do you know who consider themselves part jellyfish?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …And she says she knows you. Good catch there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, they do. I know of at least three or four meet-ups for therians/otherkin that are fairly regular, and far more that were basically one-time things. Far more simply have regular contact with each other that's offline.

However, looking at the largest directory of therians and otherkin I can find, there are maybe three to four hundred in the entire United States, which is not nearly enough to cause this sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmmm….
What if the theoretical conspiracy really doesn't exist, the lens just picks folks at random. But by lumping all the -1 together and treating them as unpromotable second-class citizens, The Foundation provokes exactly the sort of "race" conflict they are afraid of?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I know SCP-████-█...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, I can kind of see it. Completely unintentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My job here is done! *flies away dramatically*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could see a decent Tale coming from one of the canon Foundation characters being found out as an SCP-1719-1, reassigned, and trying to figure out what they think is wrong with him…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excecution nabbed it for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda sucks for foundation 1719-1 if the threat actually doesn't exist, and they just got majorly downgraded for something that's not all that dangerous.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking of the other one…but that makes a bizarre amount of sense, now that I think about it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You overestimate the normalcy of other people

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi Scantron, yeeeeahh…did you hear we're putting the new coversheets on our TPS reports now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now I will never see a camera again. Thank you very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's weird. Even knowing the image is a camera, I still see a robot knocked on its side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe to expand on my wild headcanon: the lens shows your primal envy/jealousy or just a plain fascination with animals. Liked fauns and centaurs? Currently using a wolf as a profile picture? Had sex with a snail? Told your dog that "man I wish I was like you"? The lens would show it. Pretty useless compared to what the Church believes it to be but interesting nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good one, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's been a while since I took bio, but I believe that these are phenotypes, not genetic traits.

It's probably been even longer since I took bio, so you could be right.

the Foundation's reaction- particularly the xk panic- bugs me a little

Why? According to the premise of the article, there are close to 800 million SCP-1719-1 in the world. I would expect that the Foundation considers it important to find out for sure what is going on with these people, and would be in a complete panic if they had to actually do something involving all of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it does. I like that we are up to about seven explanations of what is actually going on with this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Could it be... *potential spoiler*
- South Korea?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the best country you can think of that fits the number of black boxes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hair/eye/skin color, blood type, etc

It's been a while since I took bio, but I believe that these are phenotypes, not genetic traits.

As for the article proper, the Foundation's reaction- particularly the xk panic- bugs me a little, along with a few other things I have a hard time qualifying at the moment. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god i love/hate it when someone writes like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if the invasion is almost over, and the -1 instances are the only normal humans left?

It would make about as much sense as the other way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, you haven't considered the closet furries. Clearly, a large portion of the world are in deep denial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool idea, well executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea! The writing was also pretty damn good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that's most likely what's going on — but the Foundation can't prove it, and if there is even a 1% chance the effect is real, they are going to make contingency plans for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this idea, and the way it was executed left me thinking for a while.
Great job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this idea - myself, I think it's more effective if the -1s are genuinely just normal humans with a genetic marker that is picked up on by 1719; They Live if the glasses just made you see an alien conspiracy that wasn't actually there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really funny interpretation that I admit to not having thought of. Anomalously interpreting something psychological about some people? Sure, why not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent, I love this. Articles where the thing they're containing is an idea, more or less, are on the rare side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tell meeeeeeeeeeeeeee

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But Otherkin don't exist outside the Internet

Clearly, you haven't been around a big group of furries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But Otherkin don't exist outside the Internet, and furries aren't ubiquitous enough to be 1/10 of the population.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon: It's actually a device made by a few enthusiastic furries years ago as a way to tell if someone is a furry/otherkin/etc, and it surprisingly worked, but the engineers just thought they created a really good real-time CGI animation camera. They revealed this device at a small and not-so-popular convention and used it in a LARP. The "recovered material" is part of the larger LARP that the Church blissfully was unaware of and the founders of the Church were not aware of its origins, as the original engineers just discarded it after they moved on to much more important stuff than a furry-finder. So, instead of finding aliens they harassed a bunch of Wolf's Rain fans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it started out intending to be a straight inversion of the plot of They Live and a couple similar stories — it looks like the Foundation has uncovered a massive alien conspiracy (that you can only see through funny glasses) — but it isn't; it's just a weird illusion where the lens is reacting anomalously to something completely mundane and harmless. They would still want to contain this thing due to the potential for creating social chaos, but it's not what it seems to be.

Then I thought it would work better if the Foundation isn't sure what's going on:

It's an illusion.

It IS a massive alien conspiracy, but instead of doing the stereotypical "Hey, they can see us, let's kill them!" bit, the aliens are REALLY good liars.

There is something anomalous about the people who look "wrong," but it's not what it appears to be; this is just how the SCP-1719 device interprets some other anomaly.

There is an alien conspiracy, but it hasn't played out yet. The aliens are here, but genuinely have no idea what they are…they were left here thousands of years ago and nothing is going to happen until whatever placed them here comes back. If they come back. Maybe they just forgot.

What's really going on? You decide.

So, you see, I don't think I just SCPified They Live, if you consider the story behind the object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happily upvoting this, excellent job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just outstanding. Simple idea, executed great. +1
